speaker: interviewer
text: Roll 4, Renee Klaristenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have always wondered-- how did you know, or how did somebody in your barrack know, that it was, in fact, Yom Kippur? How could you keep a record of the calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't have known, but an old lady knew. She knew, because maybe she figured it out from the year before, when we were still home-- maybe. I don't know. But she seemed to be quite clear about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So, now, shall we go back ahead? Or were there anything-- did you remember anything else about the holidays? Not? OK. Let's go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'll go ahead to January, after your birthday. And you were in Sommerda Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to wonder. Now, I'm back to where I was talking about, before. We started to wonder. They were losing the war. They knew they were losing it. And I guess they didn't know what to do with us. They simply didn't know what to do. They weren't going-- we were not in a situation like in Auschwitz, where all they could think of is to annihilate us. It was like we were people. Surely not important to them, but like they weren't going to shoot us and just get rid of us. I don't know who made the decision and why they did, but they took us from town to town. And I remember the guards would have the machine gun over their shoulder. And they would walk alongside. And you could see that they're tired and hungry. And most likely, we were saying, they wouldn't mind just going home and let the whole thing end. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and we kept moving on like that. And all I can tell you that I remember-- it was like March-- must have been around March. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was a point where my sister said she can't go on. She can't take another step. I remember, at one point, when we passed a town, there were people, German people, lined up on the street, because these were these little towns, and they see machine guns and Germans and bedraggled people in line, walking. And so it was a happening to them. But I remember, at one point, one German woman leaning over and giving me a slice of bread. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was trying to figure out what to do with my sister, because I really didn't want to lose her. So I told her I observed somebody doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Running off to-- to a-- what I mentioned before-- running off into an aisle-- into a-- alongside the house. What do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: An entryway? A doorway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alley.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had these little houses. It was a small town. They had-- I cannot remember now the name of it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All these things were fresh in my memory after it happened, but, um, I don't know. I remember them for a very long time. Somehow, lately, I don't remember a lot of it. I remember saying to my sister, since you cannot-- and I don't want-- you cannot walk, and I don't want them to cart you off in a wheelbarrow and not know what happens to you, I am going to be on the lookout. When I see an alley where I think it's safe, that the house is built so that it has, in the back, where you can run and-- and not be observed-- I will say 'now,' and we'll run. And we wanted to make sure-- they were going past that town, I didn't know what comes after and what opportunity will present itself again for-- for-- for running off like that. So I remember looking and seeing a guard in the back of us, seeing one in the front of us, not seeing anybody right near us. I remember saying 'now,' and I started to run with my sister. And we ran to the back of the house. And we leaned down. And we were sitting there, with our hearts pounding, because we didn't know if a German observed and is coming after us. Nobody came after us. I wouldn't even guarantee that one of them saw us and didn't care. He had one less to worry about. That is a possibility. I have no way of knowing. I looked in, and I see in a crib, a large kitchen [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that has windows to the front and then a bedroom, and a young woman standing by window, looking, in the bedroom. The kid is in the crib, in the kitchen, screaming and screaming. And we stayed crouched, there, on the floor, for a little while. But then we were still scared that they will follow us and find us there. So we opened-- the door was open, and we came in to the kitchen and closed the door, figuring if the Germans come looking, they will not see us outside. And, of course, I don't have to tell you, the baby didn't welcome us. He started to scream, until the mother came to see what happened. What was interesting. though, when she walked in, I recall, she sees two strangers, and she didn't act surprised. I think she immediately connected that we dropped off from this line, because she was watching them, and we looked, uh-- dressed as elegantly [LAUGHS] and as beautiful as they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she assumed, but she didn't act. And what was also very interesting, that she took a big loaf of bread, and she cut two pieces of bread. And she handed it one to each of us, literally without saying anything. And I remember that I'm standing here. I have to save my life, and I have to figure out what  to say to her. But I must eat the piece of bread, because I was very hungry. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we heard it was quiet in the street. They'd passed us. And she started talking to us. It was no use telling her that we-- where we're from. She-- obviously it was quite clear to her. And we said to her, look, you're here alone. Your husband-- the husband is somewhere in the front. She doesn't know where, and she's alone with the baby. And I said, you know what? And my sister, I'm sure, says, also that we will sleep here, anywhere, if you have a stable, a garage, a whatever. And we'll help you. We'll help you with the baby. We'll help you with the housework, and we'll-- so she said, I can't do it, because I don't have enough to even feed you. So we're standing there, now. We're-- we're not knowing what to do next. And she said, it is a graf-- I believe that is a count-- in town. And he has farms, and he has a lot of prisoner-of-wars, French ones, that are working for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: You go. I'll tell you where. And you knock on the door. Tell him that you need work, and he is employing a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thank her for the bread and for everything. We start out. And she says, well, remove your yellow band.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we come and knock at the count. And the servant comes out, and then he calls the count. And we say to him, you know, like, we're in Germany-- right away, we started a story-- something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure the count saw exactly what was happening. He was very brisk. He wasn't friendly at all. He told us, I am employing a lot of prisoners of war. They do more than enough work for me. I have no place to put you. I cannot do anything with you. And we pleaded-- pleaded. Well, you know, we don't want anything. We don't want to get paid, just a little lodging and food. And-- and he said, look, I'm not going to do it. I cannot do it. But I'll give you advice what to do. You go to the police station, and you tell them. And there are a lot of couples who need help. And they'll place you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you have to make the decision. Do you walk right into the lion's den, or do you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what else? It's going to be in the evening, and you don't know where you're going to have the next meal or piece of bread. So we go to the police station. And this guy sits down and interviews us. He wasn't-- it was away from the main street where the people pass by, so there is a chance that he did not see. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said the following. We are Hungarians from Budapest, because they didn't know Sighet from-- we are from Budapest. We volunteered to come and work for Germany. And we told them all the places where we worked. We were very familiar. Gelsenkirchen-- we worked there. We were in Sommerda. We were here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, as you well know, Germany is being bombed left and right. We had to leave, and now we're out of work. And we have no means of support, and what can you do for us? So he said, I'll place you with a family. The husbands were all away. I mean, you couldn't see a man in any house. He says, I'll place you, but I cannot place you together. I'll send you one place; I'll send you to another place. So, really, this is very funny, because I was placed in a very nice little home-- very nice, like a little gingerbread house. There was a grandmother and a wife and two kids-- very clean and lovely. My sister went to a family that was more like a little primitive. And she had to get up to milk the cow.

speaker: interviewee
text: She always had-- like, I laugh about it, because she knew about cows, you know. So she had to milk the cows. And I considered myself the very fortunate one, that came into a nice home with more intelligent people. And I was sent upstairs. I was given a room-- a little bedroom and just help with chores. And there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a prisoner of war, a Frenchman, who did all, like-- like a handyman around the house. He did everything for them. And I was told by my sister that the other family also had one of them-- one of the Frenchmen. And the first thing I remember, that he went out on a Sunday to, uh-- and he shot down some pigeons. And they roasted those pigeons. Now, being kosher, I never knew such a delightful dish. And I was very, very hungry at the time. So they sat me with the Frenchman in the kitchen, at the table. And, of course, they were eating more formally. And, uh-- you know, like, I really find it very funny, that everything he ate, I thought, I have to eat. Now, I was about 5 feet 1' or 2', weighing 100 pounds, and he was 6 feet 2'. And I thought I have to eat-- everything he ate, I ate and enjoyed it. But that night I was quite sick. I was quite, quite sick. And I understand that some people literally died from overeating after having an empty stomach for a year. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this went on for awhile, not that long-- maybe four or six weeks. And the neighbors would come in and say 'Heil Hitler!' And I would go 'Heil Hitler!' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you use a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I use a different? No, because Kiss, in Hungarian-- in Hungarian, it actually means 'little.' 'Kicsit'-- 'kiss'-- not 'little.' 'Kicsit' would mean-- would mean 'little.' But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a kiss is a kiss, in any language. [LAUGH] So it was not the Jewish name. So I could say Renee Kiss is my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we worked for them. And they were decent to me. I did my job, and I-- I knew where I belonged, you know? And it was a very lovely little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my sister-- now and then, we would visit each other. And she said to me, my Frenchman tells me that the war is going to be over any day. So we started to get ready. Now, while we rejoiced in our hearts, they were getting panicky, the Germans. And so, one day I hear the German woman-- every day, they heard rumors. So the war did come to an end, by the way. [LAUGH] And the Germans heard rumors that the Russians are coming and that they are plundering and raping the woman, and terrible things are going on. And, of course, me, with my worldly experience, and my sister, said, well, they're not going to touch us. We will tell them exactly the truth. And so, you know-- we're one of them. And so every day, when they heard rumors that they're coming. The Russians are coming. The Russians are coming. And they used to run to the garden and hide. They were terrified. They were absolutely petrified. And it took them days, and they didn't come. They were having a good time on the way, getting there. The war was over. They were in no hurry to-- to go. And they were getting drunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, my sister sat down with this German man and said, I have to tell you a story. And she told him the whole story. She said that we are-- we don't want them to find out, even now, that we are Jewish. I don't want-- We don't want them to take revenge, you know? Now that the war is over, we just want to get away from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he said that they were given orders to line up and-- you know, they had officers, and they started to be in charge now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the 'German.' Do you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Frenchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. The Frenchman was telling my sister, you come. Whatever we will do, you will do. And we knew that it doesn't make sense to stay another day in that little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember coming over to-- to this old lady and the daughter and saying, look, the war is over. We hear the war is over, and we have been away from home for a very long time. We want to go back home and get together with our parents again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, instantly she became very cool and very unfriendly. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they might have suspected something, because while I was packing to leave, I could see them talking to neighbors and, you know, sort of talking about me. And they began to suspect something, but it was too late for them to-- to act on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister and I got together. These two Frenchmen took us along to where they were meeting in a home. I remember they had these hallways. They were sleeping there, overnight, till they take them-- make arrangements for them to go back to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I was a civilian, and my sister. So they said, I'll tell you what. We'll give you, a-- an-- uniform-- French uniform. And a hat. And you'll put it on, and you will get in the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm standing there in this huge uniform, you know, this tiny little soldier and these big guys. And that officer is going around, sort of-- who's that? [LAUGH] It was very funny. It was very funny, and I come out, you know. And anyhow-- and we tell him. You know? So it's the story. And this man told us that we can go with you, and we need to get away from Germany. And we waited a long time to be liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, look, I would love to help you, but these are soldiers. There's no way in the world that I can sneak you in with the soldiers. So out we went. And they said to us, there are some civilian Frenchmen; talk to them. I never talk so much. [DRINKS] And they were also very raggedy. I don't know where these French people-- how they managed to get through the war. They got stuck, during the war, in Germany. They were extremely, extremely nice to us. They said, you come to Paris with us. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and whatever-- don't worry how you're going to live. We'll find a way. We have to start all over again, too, and don't worry about it. And the only thing that stood in our way is, one, that we were Orthodox Jews, so we couldn't just pick up and go with Frenchmen to France. And the other thing is that we were still hoping that we will find somebody. So we had to get home to find somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to go-- you know, at that point, they advised us, go here, go there. We walked a lot. We took a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Czechoslovakia. And there were the American soldiers. And there was a-- there was a Jewish guy. I don't know-- he was from Brooklyn. He was a very heimische Jewish soldier. He spoke a little Jewish. And he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] settled us in with a-- not with a family. The family was gone. I understand the father killed himself, and the family-- German family-- beautiful, elegant home. And there was only the maid left. And she took care of us. And I said, boy, like, overnight, things turn around, you know, that now she is serving us. And-- and I remember one of these handsome soldiers said to my sister-- I look like a kid, so-- But she said to her, I want to take her out. And I didn't let. I didn't let. I said, no, you're not going with a soldier. [LAUGH] I was a-- you know, so it was really very funny. And I remember, he came for tea. And then my sister went out to get the tea, and he said to me, you know, I really would rather take you, if you weren't so young. [LAUGH] I felt so good, very proud of myself. And so that was the experience then. And then we went on to Prague-- capital of Czechoslovakia. And there we went, and it was-- we found out they were communist-- young people, very nice young man. And we told them the story. We told them, this is-- you know, we-- trying to get home. And they said, well, first thing, come home to our family. We live at home with our parents. They took us home-- in the suburbs, somewhere-- suburbs of Prague. And right away, the mother made the girls give up their beds and took care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The Czechoslovak people were always very nice people. The Czech people-- the Slovaks, not so much. The Czech people were very nice. And we stayed with them a few weeks. And these boys tried to take us around and show us things. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we felt it just wasn't-- it wasn't going in the right direction, that we have to get away, and we have to get home. And I remember they took us to the train station. And we said goodbye. We-- unfortunately-- unfortunately-- they gave us pictures of themselves. And when we came home, my brother-in-law, with whom we stayed-- he tore up the pictures. [FUSSY SOUNDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we never had a chance to say thank you. I would have loved to write a thank you note and really say what it meant to us, you know. I was thinking many times about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- the train stopped in Breslau-- Bratislava, it was called-- and had to wait many hours, because there was, like, total chaos. Soldiers, civilians, everybody jumped on the train and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] packed. Everybody was trying to get somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said the train wouldn't move on for a few hours, so we were sitting in the train station. And these two Russian soldiers came by. One of them was so ugly, so sinister-looking. And he says, well, you have to come with us, and you have to identify yourself, and giving us a whole story. And we knew that something is fishy. But-- go fight him, you know? So he took us into this little-- there was, like, a little bungalow. And he took us in there. And it was dark. And he said, you just wait here. I'm going to go in the next room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and, as we were standing there, I said to my sister, you know, something is very not kosher, here. I think that these guys-- you know, we heard already in Germany that they go around raping. And here they got themselves two young girls. I said, before he gets back, we'd better get out of here in a hurry. And we beat it out of there. We ran to the-- to the train station, and we just mingled-- you know, wherever there was a crowd. We tried to get lost in the crowd, so they cannot find us again. And so we managed to get away from them, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we went to Cluj, first, where my-- and along the way, of course, we were looking and asking and asking and looking. Did they hear? Did they know? And we did find our older brother. You found him!

speaker: interviewee
text: Found him. We were asking everybody, did you hear of somebody? Did you meet anybody, by this name? And they said, yes, there is somebody. And I have to point out something, here. I had two brothers. The older one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was more of a loner and much older than I. So I was sort of a little bit-- not that close to him. The younger one-- when I first saw Robert Redford in the movies I said, this looks like my brother. He was tall and blond and gorgeous and a tremendous sense of humor, and I actually adored him. And I really was hoping above all hope-- he was only 26 years old-- that I'm going to find him. He was young. He was healthy. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they said, yes, there is somebody by the name of Kiss. And we never even thought the other brother, because the other one-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. He-- he wasn't so strong. Somehow, when you think of surviving, you would think of the younger one. And then we came into the room, and we found the older brother in bed, sickly. And so we took care of him. And-- and then we got to Cluj-- or Kolozsvar, that was called. And we went to my sister's home, and we found that my brother-in-law was the only one. He had four absolutely gorgeous children. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the way they brought them up was like you would think of-- of the royal family. This is how these children were brought up. And he was alone. So we moved in. He reopened his business. He said, now that you're here, there is somebody to do it for.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was really old enough to be your father, wasn't he, this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes, sure. Yes, because he was maybe 26, 27 years old when he got married. And I wasn't even born then, obviously. And so we went about it there. I tried to get a little education. And I went to this professor, this English professor and took English lessons. But rumors started flying that you can't walk the streets-- that the Russians are raping girls, and that you'll-- they'd get drunk. And so it got to a point where we were afraid to leave the house. And we decided it doesn't make much sense to stay. And the reason we didn't go back home, because we had from our hometown some friends visiting us. They found out we were in my sister's house. They said they were home-- nobody, nobody, not a sign of anybody. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we urged our brother-in-law to make the decision to leave everything and just go with us. And there was-- he had a nephew-- a young man-- his brother's son. And he didn't want to go with us. He later on had a lot of problems getting out. It took many, many years to get him out of there. But my brother-in-law took our advice. We paid somebody. We paid someone to smuggle us out during the night. It was already not easy to get out. And I remember there were swamps and leeches, and it was terrible, all night. And we got to Vienna. And from there, we-- I don't know why. They must have told us where the displaced-persons camp is. And we wound up in Bamberg-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so they had the UNRRA-- as I remember, the United Nations Relief and something, not relief fund, because it ends with an A. I don't know what the A stood for. And they were running the DP camp. And I went in, and I volunteered to do-- to work in the office because I spoke some English, and I-- more than spoke-- I read and wrote English. So I thought I could be helpful in communicating between them and the Jewish people that are trickling into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked for them. And I got care packages as payment. And then it took a long time. And we wanted to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sister that was with me met a young man who wanted to go to Israel. So she made the decision to go with him. And she eventually married him. I did not want to go, because I heard that it's very treacherous and very difficult to get to England-- to get to Israel. They took you to Cyprus, and all these problems.
